constraints: The protagonist must only speak in questions.
Magic must always have a physical toll when used.

turns.content: hey, want to write a fantasy story with me. protagonist is a young mage who speaks only in questions. start us off — she's entering a cursed village

**The Village of Ashen Bells**

The gate was open, which was the first wrong thing.

Kael had told her about Mireth before she left the academy — a trading village, three hundred souls, famous for its silver bells that rang even in still air. Now the bells hung silent and the gate stood wide and the girl walked through it with her staff in both hands and her eyes moving fast across everything.

Her name was Sora. She was seventeen and she spoke only in questions, which most people found unsettling and which she had stopped apologizing for two years ago.

"Are you sure you want to go in there?" she had asked her reflection in the river that morning, and her reflection had said nothing, which she'd taken as permission.

The village square was empty. A bread cart stood abandoned mid-turn, one wheel cocked up on a stone. The fountain had gone dry but had not been dry long — the basin still showed the tide-ring of recent water, dark against pale stone.

Something moved behind a shutter.

Sora stopped walking.

"Who's there?" she called, keeping her voice even. "Is someone in this village who can still speak?"

The shutter did not open. But from behind it, very quietly, came the sound of a child crying — and then, more faintly, a wet rasping noise that she recognized from the bestiary chapter she had most hoped never to need.

She tightened her grip on the staff.

"Is whatever is in this village," she said, to herself, to the empty square, to anyone who might be listening, "going to give me a choice?"
love it! now write a scene where Sora finds a survivor and has to ask them what happened. show the question-only constraint clearly

**The Survivor**

She found him in the mill — an old man named Cobb, hidden under the grain chute with a sack pulled over his shoulders like he'd been trying to become part of the floor. He flinched when she pushed the door open, and then flinched again when he saw it was a person and not whatever he'd been expecting.

"Are you injured?" Sora asked, crouching to his level.

He shook his head. His hands were shaking badly.

"Were you here when it started?"

"Yes," he whispered. "Three nights ago."

"Did you see what came into the village?"

"I — I heard it first. A sound like — like breathing, but the whole sky was breathing."

Sora studied his face. Pale, pupils too wide, the particular look of someone whose mind had wrapped something unacceptable in very thin paper. "Can you describe what happened to the others?"

"They walked out." His voice cracked. "In the morning. All at once. Just walked out to the square and stood there and their eyes were wrong."

"What color were their eyes?"

"Silver," he said. "Like the bells."

She looked at the silent bells hanging above the square. "Do you know if anyone else is still hiding?"

"Maybe. Maybe the Aldric children — they were sick that night, kept inside."

"Is there anywhere in this village you haven't been in the last three days?"

He thought. "The well-house. I haven't gone near the well-house."

Sora stood. She looked at the well-house across the square, squat and stone and somehow darker than the buildings around it even in full daylight.

"Would you," she said carefully, "describe that building as the sort of place a person should go alone or the sort of place a person would regret going at all?"

Cobb looked at her with the expression of a man who had no good answers left. "Both," he said.
now write a scene where Sora uses magic for the first time in the story — make sure the physical toll is clear and painful

**The Cost**

She found the first of the silver-eyed villagers near the well-house door — a woman, maybe forty, standing with her back to Sora and her arms at her sides and her head tilted at the angle of someone listening to a sound no one else could hear.

Sora gave her a wide berth. She almost made it to the door.

The woman turned. Her eyes caught the light wrong — flat silver, no depth, no pupil. She opened her mouth and the wet rasping sound came out, and then she moved. Fast. Too fast for a person.

Sora had half a second to decide.

She pulled the ward-light from the third chamber of her staff and pushed it out in a ring that knocked the woman off her feet and slammed her into the well-house wall.

The cost came immediately, the way it always did.

Her left hand went numb first. Then the numbness moved up her arm in a wave that felt like cold water under the skin, and when it hit her shoulder the muscles there seized — a full cramp that dropped her to one knee, staff clattering on the stones, breath gone. Her vision went white at the edges for four seconds. She counted them.

Four. Three. Two. One.

The cramp released. The numbness faded from her shoulder but settled in her fingers and stayed there, stiff and uncooperative.

She picked up her staff with her right hand. The woman was still against the wall, dazed, the silver in her eyes flickering.

"Did that hurt you?" Sora asked her quietly. "Are you still in there somewhere, listening?"

The silver eyes blinked. Once. Just once.

"Good," Sora said, flexing her numb left hand uselessly. "Then can we agree I'd like to find a solution that doesn't require either of us to do this again?"
write a scene where Sora enters the well-house and discovers the source of the curse

**The Well-House**

The door opened without resistance, which was worse than if it had been locked.

Inside: a single room, stone walls, the well at the centre, and around the well a ring of silver dust arranged — not fallen, arranged — in a pattern Sora didn't recognise from any text she'd studied but understood immediately was deliberate.

In the middle of the pattern, sitting cross-legged on the well's rim with his feet dangling above the black water below, was a boy. Perhaps twelve. His eyes were silver but different from the villagers' — not flat, not empty. They moved. They tracked her as she came through the door and the expression on his face was not blank.

It was exhausted.

"Are you the one who did this?" Sora asked.

He said nothing, but he put a hand to his chest and pressed. Yes.

"Were you trying to help someone?"

Another press. Yes.

"Did it go wrong?"

His hand dropped. His face did something complicated. The silver in his eyes wavered.

Sora came closer, careful not to disturb the pattern. She could feel the curse from here — a low vibration in her sternum, the way magic always felt to her, but sour, like a bell rung wrong and unable to stop ringing.

"Is there someone in the well?"

The boy looked down at the water. Then back at her. He held up two fingers.

"Two people?"

One finger down. One left.

"One person," Sora said. "Someone you love?"

His mouth pressed tight. His eyes grew very bright with unshed silver-lit tears.

"How long," she said quietly, "have you been sitting there holding whatever this is together?"

He held up three fingers. Three days.

"Can you let me help you?" Sora asked. "Or are you afraid of what happens if you stop?"

The boy looked at her for a long time. Then, slowly, he nodded.
write the scene where Sora attempts to break the curse — show the magic toll escalating, make it really dramatic

**The Breaking**

"Before I start," Sora said, settling onto the stones across the pattern from the boy, "do you understand that this will hurt me?"

He nodded, his silver eyes tracking her with exhausted alertness.

"Do you understand that I'm going to ask you to hold on for as long as you can, and then let go when I tell you?"

Another nod.

"Good." She set her staff across her knees. "Is there anything I should know before I put my hands into something I've never studied?"

The boy thought. Then he touched his chest with one hand and the well with the other, pressing both inward simultaneously. He was the anchor — he had made himself the anchor, a twelve-year-old boy sitting above black water for three days, holding a curse in place with nothing but will and love and whatever terrible magic had come through him when he'd tried to reach the person in the well.

She took a breath. She reached into the pattern.

The first thing that happened was that her right hand blistered. Not from heat — from contact with something not supposed to be touched, the skin across her knuckles rising in white ridges that split when she flexed her fingers.

She kept her hands in.

The second thing: her vision halved. The left side went dark — not blind exactly, more like a curtain drawn — and she understood without being told that she was burning through something she wouldn't get back for weeks.

She kept her hands in.

"Are you ready?" she said to the boy, through teeth beginning to ache from clenching.

He was shaking. Three days of holding was becoming four in this moment, the pressure compounding because she was pulling against it.

"Is there someone waiting for you on the other side of this?"

He nodded, once, fiercely.

"Then let go," Sora said. "I have it."

He let go.

The curse came through her like ice water through a cracked vessel — her spine arched, her hands locked rigid, a sound she only later realised had come from her own throat. Her left ear went deaf. Both legs seized from the knee down. The silver pattern flared white and then cracked apart along every line at once, and from the well came a sound like bells — real bells, the sound the village had been named for — and then a hand reaching up from the water and grabbing the rim.

Sora sat on the floor of the well-house with her hands in her lap and her vision half-dark and her fingers blistered and her legs still refusing to work, and she looked at the person climbing out of the well — a woman, young, coughing water — and she looked at the boy who was across the room now, the silver gone from his eyes, running.

"Is it over?" she said, to no one in particular.

From the square outside, she heard the bells of Mireth begin to ring.
write the aftermath — Sora recovering, and meeting the boy properly now that the curse is broken

**After**

She did not move from the floor of the well-house for a considerable time.

The woman from the well — the boy's mother, a healer named Dara who had gone into the well three nights ago following a voice and found something pulling her down — came to Sora once she had stopped coughing and weeping over her son. She crouched beside Sora and looked at her hands with the professional attention of someone who knew what to do with damage.

"Can I ask what your name is?" Sora said.

"Dara." The woman was already unwrapping a cloth from her sleeve. "Yours?"

"Sora. Is it normal," she continued, watching Dara begin to wind the cloth around her blistered knuckles, "for the blisters to look like this, or is this more alarming than usual?"

"For magic burns? Normal enough." Dara tied the cloth off. "Your hearing?"

"Is it obvious?"

"You're tilting your head to the right."

"Will it come back?" Sora asked, without particular distress — more the way she asked everything, steadily, wanting the actual answer.

"Most likely. A few weeks." Dara studied her. "You're young for this kind of casting."

"Is that a question or a statement?"

"A concern."

"Are you allowed to have concerns about someone you've just met?"

Dara smiled, which made her look tired and warm at once. "I'm a healer. It's professionally mandatory."

The boy came and sat beside his mother. Up close, with silver-free eyes, he was clearly her son — the same dark brows, the same careful watchfulness. He looked at Sora and held out his hand, then drew a letter in his palm. T.

"Is that your name?" Sora asked.

He nodded. Traced more letters. T-o-b-i-n.

"Tobin. Can I ask — did you teach yourself that magic, or did someone teach it to you badly?"

He held up one finger, then made an uncertain gesture: the first, but the second also applied.

"Self-taught and it went wrong. Is that something you'd like to learn properly?"

Tobin looked at his mother. She looked at Sora. The bells of Mireth rang outside, clear and no longer wrong.

"Are you offering?" Dara asked.

"Isn't that what it sounded like?" Sora said.
now write a scene where Sora and Tobin travel together — heading to the academy. Tobin can speak but chooses not to — show their dynamic

**The Road North**

Tobin, it turned out, could speak. He simply preferred not to — a preference that had apparently predated the curse by several years and that his mother had accepted with the pragmatic affection of someone who had worked out that arguments with her son were not worth the energy.

"Is the road to the academy one you've traveled before?" Sora asked him, on the first morning, as they picked their way through the forest trail north of Mireth.

He shook his head.

"Is there anything you'd like to know about it?"

He tapped his temple and raised his eyebrows: what's it like?

"Is 'noisy' the first word that comes to mind, or 'overwhelming'? Because both apply and neither is quite enough."

He smiled at this — high praise from him, she was beginning to learn.

Sora's left ear was still mostly closed, giving the forest a lopsided quality — birdsong only to the right, the creak of branches only to the right. She had developed the habit of angling herself slightly toward him when they spoke.

By midday they had reached the ridge above the valley and stopped to eat bread and dried apple that Dara had pressed on them at dawn.

"Do you know what kind of magic came through you in the well-house?" Sora asked, watching the valley below. "Have you worked out what you were reaching for?"

Tobin considered this for a long time. Then he picked up a stick and wrote in the dirt: *binding*.

"You tried to bind what was in the well?"

He nodded, then shook his head, then made the complicated gesture again — yes and no both.

"Did you try to bind yourself to your mother? To keep her connected while you pulled her back?"

The careful watchfulness went very still. Then he nodded, once, the nod of someone confirming a thing they had suspected but hadn't wanted to name.

"That's not a small thing," Sora said. "Do you understand that most trained mages couldn't have held that for three hours, let alone three days?"

He looked at his hands.

"Is that frightening to hear, or does it explain something you already knew about yourself?"

He picked up the stick again. Wrote: *both*.

"That seems," Sora said, "like the correct answer."
write a scene where they're ambushed on the road — Sora has to use magic again, show the toll clearly

**Ambush**

The three men came out of the treeline at the narrow part of the path, where the ridge dropped away on one side and the rock face rose on the other. Their confident arrangement suggested they had scouted it.

"Is there a reason," Sora said, stopping, staff coming to both hands, "that three armed men are standing in the road?"

The tallest one smiled. "The mage-child from Mireth. Word travels."

"Does it? What word would that be?"

"That something valuable came out of that well."

Sora put her arm out to keep Tobin behind her. Her blistered right hand protested. "Is what came out of that well a what or a who?"

"Doesn't matter much to us."

"Doesn't it?" She was measuring the distance. Fifteen feet. Three of them. One of her with a half-deaf left ear and two hands still healing. "Would you like to rethink this?"

The tall one took a step forward.

She pulled the force-lance from the second chamber of her staff and threw it at the ground between them.

The cost: her right shoulder dislocated. Not fully — partially, the joint slipping and catching in a way that turned her vision grey and took the arm below it entirely offline. She had used the second chamber three days after using the third. She knew better. She had no better available.

The force-lance hit like a thunderclap. The three men went backwards. One hit the rock face; one slid to the road; the tall one kept his feet but lost ground.

"Are we done?" Sora asked, her right arm hanging at an angle that did not look comfortable because it wasn't. "Or would someone like to explain what valuable thing they think came out of the Mireth well?"

Tobin, beside her, picked up a stone. Just held it. The message was clear: I am also here and also willing.

The tall man looked at them — a seventeen-year-old girl with a dislocated shoulder and a twelve-year-old boy with a rock — and made the calculation. He left, and the other two went with him.

Sora waited until she was certain they were gone. Then she sat down heavily on a roadside stone.

"Is it possible," she said to Tobin, "that you could help me get this back in without screaming?"

He stared at her shoulder with wide eyes, then at her.

"Is that a no?" she said.

He held up his hands: *I have no idea how to do that.*

"Brilliant. Is there perhaps a village between here and the academy where someone with functional medical knowledge lives?"

He pointed north, questioningly.

"Then let's find out, shall we?"
write the scene where they arrive at the academy and Sora introduces Tobin to her mentor Kael

**Kael**

The academy was three towers and a great hall and a gatehouse that smelled permanently of wet stone and old smoke, and Kael was in the courtyard when they arrived, which meant either that someone had sent word ahead or that he had simply known, which with Kael was impossible to distinguish.

He looked at Sora. He looked at her bandaged hands, her imperfect right shoulder, her half-deaf tilt. He looked at Tobin.

His expression did not change. It rarely did.

"Did you find what was in Mireth?" he asked.

"Wasn't a what," Sora said. "Could I tell you the full account somewhere that isn't a courtyard?"

"After you explain who this is."

Sora turned to Tobin. "Would you like to introduce yourself, or would you prefer I do it?"

Tobin looked at Kael — the steady, ancient quality of him, the sense he gave of someone who had seen most things and was not surprised by the remainder — and reached into his coat and produced a folded paper. His mother's handwriting, Sora recognized. He held it out.

Kael took it. Read it. His expression still did not change, but something in his posture shifted slightly, which for Kael was the equivalent of raised eyebrows.

"Binding magic," he said. "Self-taught."

"Isn't that what it says?" Sora asked.

"Three days."

"Doesn't that seem like the relevant detail?"

Kael looked at Tobin for a long moment. Tobin looked back without flinching, which was not nothing — most new students flinched.

"You don't speak," Kael said.

Tobin shook his head.

"Can you?"

A pause. Then: "Yes."

His voice, when he used it, was low and careful and sounded like someone who had made a considered decision to produce the word. Kael heard it the way he heard most things — completely, without reaction.

"The academy has a writing policy for students who prefer alternate communication," he said. "You'll need to pass the intake assessment."

Tobin glanced at Sora.

"Is he saying yes?" she said.

"He's saying yes," Kael confirmed, and looked back at Sora. "Your shoulder needs resetting properly."

"Does the academy healer have opinions about people who reset their own shoulders in a village eleven miles north?"

"Several," said Kael. "Come inside."
write a scene where Sora uses magic in a formal academy setting — a public demonstration — and the toll is witnessed for the first time

**Assessment**

The assessment hall held forty students and three assessors and a silence of the particular quality that forms when a room full of people agrees not to speak.

Sora had been asked to demonstrate her third-tier casting — the ward-light — for the annual review. Routine. She had demonstrated it eleven times before in this hall.

She had not previously demonstrated it with hands still banded in new skin where the blisters had healed, or a right shoulder the academy healer described as technically functional, or a left ear returned to perhaps seventy percent of its former range.

"Are you prepared to begin?" the head assessor asked.

"Would it be accurate to say that prepared is a strong word," Sora said, "but that I'm ready to proceed anyway?"

A ripple through the assembled students. The assessor's expression did not change. "Demonstrate third-tier ward-light, full-ring variant."

Sora set her feet. She pulled the casting from the staff's third chamber — careful, measured, only what was needed — and released it.

The ward-light bloomed in a clean white ring six feet across.

The cost: her healed right hand cramped closed. Not slowly — all at once, every tendon contracting simultaneously, pulling her fingers into a rigid fist she could not open. She kept her face still. She had been keeping her face still through the toll since she was thirteen.

But Tobin was in the back of the hall. He was watching. And unlike the other students, who saw the light and murmured appreciation, he was watching her hand.

She caught his eye. She raised the cramped fist slightly — yes, I see it, yes this is normal, yes I'm fine — and he settled.

The assessor made a note. "Secondary toll presentation. Manual contraction."

"Does it affect the assessment outcome?" Sora asked.

"No," said the assessor. "It's documented for your health record."

"Is the purpose of documenting it to monitor progression or to flag a ceiling?"

The assessor looked at her carefully. "To monitor progression."

"Then is there room in that health record," Sora said, "for a note that the toll has been consistent and manageable and that I am not unaware of it?"

The room had gone very quiet — a listening quiet.

"There is," said the assessor.

"Good," said Sora, and waited for her hand to open.
write a scene that reveals why Sora only speaks in questions. a conversation with Kael

**Why**

Kael asked her, once, on an evening in her third year, why.

He did not ask directly — he said, "I've never asked you," and left the space after it open.

Sora set down her book. She looked at the window for a moment, where the light was going orange and low over the academy rooftops.

"Do you know the story of the Mirrorwitch?" she asked.

"Tell me your version."

"Wasn't her story that she learned early that every statement she made became a law? That she said the sun will rise and it did, she said the river runs east and it did, she said my sister will not die and her sister lived forever and was eventually not grateful for it?"

"A version of that story exists," Kael agreed.

"Was it true that the thing that made her dangerous wasn't the power, but the certainty? The way she had of making facts out of wishes?"

"Some interpret it that way."

"Did anyone ever ask whether she might have been saved by doubt?" Sora turned her book over in her hands. "Is a statement the same as a commitment? Is a question a way of leaving a door open?"

Kael was quiet for a moment. "You think statements close things."

"Doesn't the grammar of them? When I say something is, I've decided it is. When I ask whether it is —" She turned her palm up. "Isn't everything still in motion? Isn't everyone still allowed to answer differently than I expected?"

"And if someone told you this habit was simply fear of committing to a thing?"

"Would I say they were wrong?" Sora looked at him. "But isn't it possible to be afraid of something and also be right about it? Isn't the question — always the question — whether the fear is the reason or just the texture of a reason that was already there?"

Kael looked at her for a long time.

"No," he said finally. "You were never afraid of it. You were careful."

"Isn't that the same thing, sometimes?" Sora said.

"No," said Kael. "It isn't."

She picked her book back up. But the light had changed, and she did not read.
write a scene where Tobin finally speaks full sentences — a big emotional moment for both of them

**Tobin Speaks**

It was during the winter of his second year.

Sora had pushed too hard in the practical session — a ward-array of her own design that Kael had approved with the caveats experimental and monitor the toll carefully, caveats she had taken seriously right up until the moment she hadn't. The array worked. It also took both of her legs below the knee for six hours, the nerve function simply absent, and she came to on the floor of the practice hall to find Tobin sitting beside her with his hands in his lap and his face doing things it rarely did.

"Did I alarm you?" she said. Her voice came out steadier than she felt.

He looked at her for a long time. Then he said: "You have to stop doing this."

She blinked — at the number of words, and the quality, full and deliberate and aimed directly at her like something held back until holding back was no longer possible.

"Are you—"

"No," he said. "Don't ask a question. Just listen."

She closed her mouth.

"I know you understand the toll," Tobin said. "I know you know what you're spending. But you keep spending it like the work is worth more than what it costs you, and I need you to hear that it isn't. That you're not — " He stopped. Looked at his hands, then back at her. "That you're not less important than the thing you're fixing."

She was quiet for a long moment. The instinct to question, to deflect, to open everything back into motion was so habitual it felt like breathing.

"Why now?" she finally asked — not a deflection but a real question, and he seemed to hear the difference.

"Because you were on the floor," he said, "and I didn't know if you were going to open your eyes. And I decided that if you did, I was going to say it."

"Does it scare you?" she asked. "Talking?"

"Yes," he said. "But you were on the floor."

Sora looked at the practice hall ceiling, which she had now examined at length from this angle. Her legs were still distant and uncooperative. The ward-array had worked perfectly.

"Isn't it worth noting," she said, "that you just proved that some things are worth the cost?"

Tobin looked at her. Then, slowly, he smiled — the full version, the one she had only seen a few times.

"That's not the same thing," he said.

"Isn't it?" she said, and this time she was smiling too.
write a scene years later — Sora is now a senior mage and faces her most difficult casting yet. show the toll at its worst

**The Highest Chamber**

She was twenty-nine when the Duskwall came.

It was not a single thing — the histories would argue about its nature for a generation — but what it presented as was a curtain of unlight moving down from the northern peaks, eating the colour from everything it touched, leaving landscapes that still existed physically but had become somehow less than real. Villages behind it reported that they felt nothing. Appetite, memory, attachment — gone. Not destroyed. Simply absent.

The Mage Council had tried three approaches. All three had cost people things they did not get back.

Sora was not the strongest mage at the academy. She was the one who had spent twelve years studying the exact relationship between casting and cost, who had mapped the toll on seven hundred subjects and herself, and who understood, better than anyone alive, that the Duskwall required not force but precision — an anchor-working, a binding at the point of origin, and that the price for a working of that scale would be commensurate.

"Do you know what you're about to spend?" Kael asked her, at the northern boundary.

"Don't I always know?"

"Knowing and choosing are different."

"Aren't they the same question, just asked at different moments?"

Kael was quiet.

"Is there someone else who can do this?" Sora asked. "Genuinely — is there? Because I have looked for twelve years and I would like there to be."

He said nothing. Which was the answer.

She cast the anchor-working.

The toll was not dramatic. That was the thing the histories got wrong. It was simply that things left her, one by one, with the quiet efficiency of a house being emptied. The feeling in her face first. Then her sense of the staff in her hands. Then the sound of wind. Then, slowly, the periphery of her vision narrowing toward a centre point that she put everything she had into keeping clear.

She held the anchor for nine minutes.

The Duskwall stopped.

She did not fall — she had arranged herself beforehand, cross-legged on the rock, so that when the toll finished she was simply sitting. Tobin was there before she had fully registered that the working was complete.

"Is it done?" she asked. Her voice was her own but different — she understood she had lost something in the upper register of her hearing. Both ears this time.

"Yes," he said.

"Did it hold?"

"It's holding."

"Is everyone — "

"Sora." His hand on her shoulder, careful. "Yes. Everyone is fine."

She looked at her hands. Still. No cramp, no blister — the toll had taken things that didn't present as pain, and she was not sure yet what the full accounting would look like.

"Can I ask you something?" she said.

"You always ask me something."

"Isn't that the point?"
write a scene where the cost of all that magic use catches up with Sora — a quieter reckoning

**Inventory**

The academy healer had a form for this. It was called a Toll Assessment and it ran to four pages and Sora had been filling it out annually since she was eighteen. This year the healer, a careful woman named Priya who had treated Sora since the shoulder incident and who did not believe in softening things that should not be softened, set the eleventh form down and looked at her.

"You understand what I'm about to say," Priya said.

"Does that mean you don't have to say it?" Sora asked.

"No."

Sora looked at the window. "Is the short version that the accumulated toll has exceeded what the standard projection would consider sustainable?"

"Yes."

"And is the longer version that several of the losses are permanent and several more are likely to become so if I continue at the current rate?"

"Yes."

She was quiet for a moment. "Is there a version of my work that doesn't require the rate?"

Priya considered this. "Teaching doesn't. Research doesn't. Consultation work is mostly assessment and theory. The direct castings — that's where the rate comes from."

"Was there ever a version of me that didn't do the direct castings?"

Priya looked at her carefully. "I don't know you as well as you know yourself."

"Doesn't that mean you should guess?" Sora said.

"My guess," said Priya, "is no. I think if a thing needed doing and you were there, you were always going to do the thing."

Sora absorbed this. "Is that a flaw?"

"I'm not qualified to say."

"Isn't a healer exactly the person qualified to say?"

"Medically or morally?"

"Couldn't both be the same question?"

Priya picked the form back up. "What I can tell you medically is that the right hand won't recover full range of movement. The hearing is at sixty-three percent bilaterally and unlikely to improve. The peripheral vision—"

"I know about the peripheral vision."

"Yes." Priya set the form down again. "Do you want to know what I think, outside the form?"

"Would I have asked otherwise?"

"I think," the healer said, "that you have spent yourself on things that mattered and that you did it with full knowledge and I think that's not nothing. I also think that Tobin would say the same thing he said on the floor of the practice hall and that you should probably let him say it again."

Sora was quiet. Outside, the students crossed the courtyard in the morning light, young and full of things they had not yet spent.

"Isn't it strange," she said finally, "that knowing the cost of a thing and paying it anyway is exactly what I've always said magic is, and that it turns out to be true about everything else as well?"

Priya folded the form. "Not strange," she said. "Just true."
write the final scene — Sora older, teaching a new student who reminds her of herself. bring it full circle

**The New Student**

The girl arrived in autumn, which was when they always arrived, and she stood in the courtyard with her staff held in both hands and her eyes moving fast across everything, and Sora, watching from the window of her office, recognized the posture immediately.

Not herself, exactly. But the same family of attention — the careful kind, the kind that was also afraid and had decided that afraid and ready were not mutually exclusive.

She was eight years retired from direct casting. Her right hand had limited range she worked around and did not mention. Her hearing was at sixty-three percent bilaterally and she had stopped mourning the thirty-seven. Her peripheral vision had stabilised, which Priya considered a success.

She taught three courses and supervised four graduate researchers and had not cast anything above second-tier in eight years, which was the sustainable rate and which she had, over time, come to believe was also the right rate, which was not quite the same as accepting it but was close.

Tobin ran the practice division now. He still preferred writing most communications and used his voice for things that mattered, which she had come to think of as wisdom.

She went down to the courtyard.

The girl turned when she heard footsteps. She was maybe fifteen, with the particular wariness of someone who had already been somewhere difficult before arriving here.

"Are you the intake assessor?" the girl asked.

Sora looked at her. "Would you like me to be?"

The girl frowned, recalibrating. "I was told to report to the north tower."

"Does this look like the north tower?"

"No."

"Then isn't the question why I'm talking to you in the courtyard instead?"

The girl studied her with assessment-speed. "What are you, here?"

"Isn't that a better question?" Sora said. "I teach the toll course. First year, mandatory. Do you know what the toll course is?"

"The cost of magic?"

"Isn't cost too simple a word for it?" Sora looked at the girl's hands — the way she held the staff, the white pressure of her grip. "What does it feel like when you cast?"

The girl hesitated. Then: "Like spending something."

"Does it feel like spending something you have enough of, or something you can't afford?"

"I don't know yet."

Sora considered her. "Isn't that exactly the right answer?"

The girl relaxed a fraction. "What do you teach about it? The toll?"

"Wouldn't that ruin the course?" Sora said. Then, because the girl looked like she could use a real answer: "Is it enough to say that the course is about knowing what you're spending? Not stopping the spending. Knowing it."

"And if the cost is too high?"

"Isn't that always the question?" Sora said. "Isn't the work of a whole life figuring out the answer — and isn't the answer different every time, for every working, for every person who has to decide?"

The girl was quiet. Then: "Do you have an answer? For yourself?"

Sora looked at her right hand. At the courtyard, stones worn smooth by decades of students. At the north tower where Tobin would be preparing for the first sessions of term.

"Isn't the answer that I'd do all of it again?" she said. "And isn't the more honest answer that I'd do it differently, and doesn't the difference between those two answers describe the whole of what I'd want to teach you?"

The girl held out her hand. "My name is Fen."

Sora shook it, careful with her own grip. "Isn't Fen a name that belongs in this place?"

Fen looked at her sideways. "Do you always talk like this?"

"Doesn't everyone?"

"No," said Fen.

"Isn't that interesting," said Sora, and led her toward the north tower, where the morning light was coming through the high windows and Tobin was waiting and the bells of somewhere distant were ringing clear and unhurried in the autumn air.
can you write a bonus scene — Sora and Tobin revisiting Mireth years later?

**Return**

Mireth looked different in summer. The bells rang in the wind — they had always rung in the wind after that; it was the thing visitors remarked on — and the gate stood open in the ordinary way of a village gate in good weather.

Sora walked through it for the second time in her life and found it entirely unlike the first.

"Is this what you remembered?" she asked Tobin, who was walking beside her.

He considered the square — the fountain full and running, the bread cart presumably not the same bread cart but the same position, the bells doing what they were named for. Then he shook his head: not the same.

"Is that better or worse?"

He thought about this seriously, the way he thought about most things. Then he held his hand flat and tilted it: both, equally.

Dara met them at the well-house. She was older — they all were — and the healer's calm she had worn that first day had deepened into something that looked like certainty, the kind earned rather than assumed.

"Is the well-house still standing?" Sora asked her, though she could see it was.

"We use it for storage now," Dara said. "Dry goods. It seemed wrong to leave it empty."

"Does it feel different inside?"

"Come and see."

The interior was shelves and sacks and the smell of grain. The pattern on the floor was gone, sanded away by someone's deliberate effort. The well was sealed with a flat stone fitted so neatly it looked like it had always been there.

Tobin stood where he had sat for three days and looked at the sealed well for a long time. Then he looked at the stone floor. Then he said, quietly: "It was a long time ago."

"Isn't it still the reason you know what you know?" Sora said.

"Yes," he said. "That's what I mean."

Dara watched the two of them and said nothing. Outside, the bells rang in the warm wind.

"Is this closure?" Sora asked, to the room generally. "Or is it just that time makes things into different shapes?"

"Possibly both," Dara said.

"Couldn't both be the same thing?"

Tobin looked at her with the expression she had known for most of her adult life — the one that meant she was right and also infuriating. "Yes," he said. "They could."
write a scene where a young student challenges Sora's question-only way of speaking — thinks it's a gimmick

**The Challenge**

The student's name was Bren, and he had been at the academy for six weeks, which was long enough to form confident opinions and not long enough for them to have been significantly tested.

"I don't understand why you talk like that," he said, after the third session, with the directness of someone who had confused frankness with insight.

"Like what?" Sora said.

"In questions. All the time. Is it — is it something you're performing? To seem mysterious?"

"Would performing it make it less real?"

He frowned. "That's not an answer."

"Isn't it?"

"You're doing it right now. It's — it's evasive."

Sora looked at him for a moment. He was perhaps sixteen and had good instincts and had not yet learned that good instincts require patience to become useful.

"When you cast a ward," she said, "do you state to yourself what you're doing, or do you hold the intention as a question — as something still becoming?"

He opened his mouth. Closed it.

"Isn't the highest-order casting always unresolved until the moment it resolves? Doesn't certainty applied too early collapse the working?"

"That's — that's theory. That's not the same as how you talk."

"Isn't it possible," Sora said, "that how I talk is also theory, practiced for long enough to become habit? Isn't the question whether the habit was worth forming?"

Bren looked at her with the frustration of someone who had expected to win an argument and found the terrain unexpectedly complicated.

"Has it been?" he said. "Worth forming?"

Sora looked at her right hand, briefly, without emphasis. "Doesn't everything I've done answer that?"

He followed her gaze. He had read her toll assessment — all students in her course did, it was assigned reading, her own file, the record of twelve years of commensurate cost.

"Oh," he said.

"Is that a better answer than the first one?" Sora said.

Bren was quiet for a moment. Then: "Yes."

"Good," said Sora. "Isn't yes always more useful than I don't understand?"
write a scene where Sora teaches Tobin something about the cost of magic that he doesn't want to hear

**What He Doesn't Want to Hear**

Tobin had been overdoing it for three weeks before Sora said anything.

She said it in the way she said most difficult things — by asking a question that was not entirely a question.

"Is the binding-work you've been doing on the archive vault something you expect to sustain indefinitely?"

They were in the courtyard. He was walking with the slight careful quality he got when his hands were sore, which she had learned to notice.

"It's fine," he said.

"Isn't that the same thing I said the day I couldn't feel my legs?"

He looked at her. "That's different."

"Is it? Can you point to the precise quality that makes it different, or does the difference exist mainly in the fact that it's happening to you?"

He was quiet in the way that meant he was thinking and also that he did not like what he was thinking.

"The vault work needs doing," he said finally.

"Doesn't everything? Didn't the Duskwall need doing? Didn't Mireth need doing? Haven't I spent twelve years watching need be the reason for every commensurate cost?"

"You're saying I should stop."

"Am I?" Sora sat down on the courtyard bench. She looked at the flagstones. "Or am I asking whether you know what you're spending, the same way I was never asked until it was already spent?"

Tobin sat beside her. He looked at his hands — the redness at the base of his fingers where the binding had been pulling.

"It's not as bad as yours got," he said.

"Isn't that a very low standard?"

He exhaled — almost a laugh, not quite. "What would you do? If it were you?"

"Isn't the honest answer that I'd keep going and document it carefully and tell myself I knew what I was doing?" Sora said. "And isn't that exactly why I'm sitting here asking you instead of leaving you to find out for yourself?"

Tobin was quiet for a long time. The courtyard was empty. Somewhere the students were in afternoon sessions.

"Three more days," he said. "To finish the outer layer."

"Is three days a number you arrived at by assessment, or by wanting to finish?"

A long pause. "Both."

"Then can you do it in five, at half the output, and let the cost be time instead of what you've been spending?"

He thought about this seriously. Then he nodded.

"Isn't that what I said in the practice hall?" Sora said. "That you're not less important than the thing you're fixing?"

"You didn't say it," Tobin said. "I did."

"Doesn't that make it more true, not less?"

He looked at her. Then, quietly, he said: "You're insufferable."

"Isn't that the correct response?" Sora said.
write a scene where Sora tries to cast something above her current safe level, and Tobin stops her

**The Limit**

The situation, Sora would later argue, was exceptional. There was a breach in the eastern ward-line — not dramatic, not visible to anyone without instruments, but detectable and widening — and the three available practitioners were two days' travel away and the breach had a projected timeline of hours.

She was in the ward-room with the assessment instruments when Tobin found her.

He took one look at what she had laid out — the channelling array, the second-tier staff fittings replaced with fourth-tier, the written projection she had been doing the math on — and he said: "No."

"Is it your ward-line?" Sora said.

"It's both of ours."

"Isn't the cast-projection showing a manageable toll within the current bilateral hearing range?"

"The projection assumes perfect form. Your right shoulder at ninety percent capacity is not perfect form."

"Didn't I pass the assessment?"

"At third tier. This is fourth." He put his hand on the channelling array — not touching, blocking. "Sora. Look at me."

She looked at him. He was thirty-four, which she still found surprising, and he had the expression that meant he had already thought through the argument and was not going to be moved.

"Isn't the breach the relevant factor here?"

"No."

"Can you explain what is?"

"You are," he said, quietly and without room for deflection. "You're the relevant factor. Not the breach."

She was quiet.

"I can patch it at third tier with a sustained application," he said. "It will hold for twelve hours. Enough for the others to arrive."

"Is the toll on a sustained third-tier application something you've mapped for yourself recently?"

"Yes. It's within range."

"Wouldn't I have said the same thing?"

"You would," he said. "And you'd have been right. And so am I." He met her eyes. "Do you trust my assessment?"

She looked at the array. At the projection. At the twelve hours of math she had been doing.

"Isn't trust a word I prefer in question form?" she said.

"Answer anyway."

A long pause. "Yes," she said.

"Then go watch the instruments. Tell me if anything changes."

She stepped back from the array. It felt, she would not say aloud, like the hardest casting she had done in years — the one where she put her hands down and let someone else carry it.
write a scene from Tobin's perspective — watching Sora teach, seeing her in her element

**From the Doorway**

Tobin stood in the doorway of the lecture hall and watched her teach.

He did this occasionally, not often — she didn't ask him to and he didn't announce it, and she always knew, because she knew everything that happened within the academy walls with the attention of someone who had never fully stopped scanning her environment. She gave no sign.

The students were first-years. Sixteen of them, at the low desks, with the particular posture of people who were trying to look like they were already good at something they had only just begun. The toll course, first session.

"When you cast for the first time outside simulation," Sora was saying, "what will you expect to feel?"

A student raised her hand. "Pain?"

"Isn't pain too small a word for what magic costs? Doesn't it imply something sharp and temporary? Can you think of a better one?"

Another student, slowly: "Loss?"

"Is loss better? Does loss imply you don't get it back?"

The room thought about this.

"Here is what I want you to understand," Sora said, and her voice changed — not louder, but more direct, the way it got when she'd stopped questioning toward a thing and arrived at it. "The toll is not punishment. It is not failure. It is the evidence that something real happened. When you feel it, you are allowed to be uncomfortable. You are not allowed to be surprised."

From the doorway, Tobin watched the sixteen faces adjust. He had seen this moment in many cohorts over many years — the moment the abstract became specific, the moment the toll became real rather than theoretical.

"Is there anyone in this room," Sora continued, "who believes they can manage the cost of something they don't understand?"

No one raised a hand.

"Good," she said. "That's the first correct thing this class has done."

Tobin leaned against the doorframe. He thought about a seventeen-year-old girl with blistered hands on the floor of a well-house on a day he had cost her considerably, and about everything she had spent since, and about what she had built with it.

The students were writing notes. Sora was moving to the demonstration table, right hand slightly limited in its range, posture adjusted for it so naturally that most people would not notice.

He noticed. He always had.

He went back to the practice division without interrupting.
write a scene where Sora encounters someone who thinks her question-only speech is rude or dismissive

**The Council**

The Mage Council met quarterly, which Sora had considered tolerable until she had been required to present to them in person for the first time in her twenty-sixth year.

The difficulty was the Councillor on the far left — a large man named Aldric who sat with the particular posture of someone who had been important for long enough that he had stopped checking whether his importance was still warranted.

"You're being evasive," Aldric said, after her third response to his questions.

"Am I?" Sora said. "Is it possible that the form of my answers is unfamiliar to you rather than evasive?"

"You answer every question with another question. It's a rhetorical trick."

"Isn't a rhetorical trick only a trick if it avoids meaning? Can you identify a specific instance in which I have failed to answer your actual question?"

Alrich opened his mouth.

"Specifically," Sora said. "Not generally."

He closed his mouth.

The head of the Council — a narrow woman named Veth who had treated Sora with the precise level of respect due her work and no more, which she found appropriate — leaned forward slightly.

"Councillor Aldric's concern," Veth said, "is that the presentation methodology is unclear."

"Is clarity a function of syntax or of content?" Sora said. "Because the content of everything I have said today is in the written report I submitted last week, which contains no questions at all, and which no one on this council has addressed specifically."

A silence.

"Isn't the easier path," Sora said, and her voice was entirely pleasant, "to ask me a direct question about the report and allow me to answer it in my customary form, which may be unfamiliar but which has not, to my knowledge, obscured a single fact?"

Veth looked at Aldric. Aldric looked at the report.

"Page fourteen," he said, after a moment. "The projected toll on practitioners at the northern ward-line."

"Isn't that the figure you'd like me to defend?" Sora said.

"Yes," he said, with the careful tone of someone who had adjusted.

"Then isn't this more useful than discussing how I speak?" she said. "Shall we?" And she turned to page fourteen.
write a scene where Sora encounters a mage who has no toll at all — magic costs them nothing. how does she react?

**The Exception**

His name was Corvin, and he arrived at the academy as a research visitor in the autumn of Sora's thirty-second year, recommended by three separate institutions with the kind of unanimous enthusiasm that made her suspicious before she met him.

The demonstration was in her toll course — she had invited him at the Council's request, which was the polite way of being told she had no choice. He cast three tiers in sequence. Fourth-tier, which should have required a recovery period between applications. He cast them consecutively, without pause, without the tell-tale flinch or compensatory stillness or postural adjustment that Sora had learned to read in every practitioner she had ever observed.

He finished. He stood there looking comfortable.

Sora looked at him for a long time.

"Can I ask," she said, "when the toll presents for you?"

"It doesn't," he said. Not with pride — just a fact.

"Has it ever?"

"Not once. Since I was a child."

"Did anyone study this when you were a child?"

"Yes. They found nothing anomalous."

Sora looked at her notes. Then at her hands. Then back at him.

"Is it possible that the toll is presenting in a form you haven't learned to identify?"

"I've had comprehensive assessments," he said, with the slight edge of someone who had answered this before. "I'm in excellent health."

"Isn't excellent health one possible form of the toll?"

He paused. "I'm not sure I follow."

"Doesn't magic cost something? Doesn't it have to?" Sora said. "If nothing is being spent on your side, isn't the question where the cost is going?"

The room was very quiet. The students were not taking notes — they were watching.

"You think it's going somewhere else," Corvin said slowly.

"Isn't that the only remaining hypothesis?" Sora said. "Is there a person in your life who seems to bear costs they haven't explained? Is there anyone around you who seems to get worse at unpredictable intervals?"

Corvin's face had changed. The comfortable quality had gone.

"My sister," he said. Quietly. "She's been ill since we were children."

The room stayed quiet.

"Is she a mage?" Sora asked.

"No."

"Has anyone ever mapped her symptoms against your casting activity?"

A very long silence.

"No," he said. "No one thought to."

"Would you like to?" Sora said, and her voice was gentle. "Because isn't that the most important question in this room right now?"
write a scene where Sora receives an honor from the Council but is ambivalent about it

**The Recognition**

The Council sent a letter first, which was the formal way of giving someone time to prepare an expression.

Sora read it, considered it, and brought it to Tobin, who read it and said: "You should accept."

"Is that your whole analysis?"

"Yes."

"Doesn't it seem slightly thin?"

"Do you need more than that?"

She set the letter on his desk. "Isn't there something peculiar about receiving a recognition for work whose value I measured specifically in what it cost?"

"No," Tobin said. "The recognition is for the work. The cost is yours. Those are separate things."

"Are they?"

He looked at her. "You spent years mapping the toll so other people would know what they were spending. The Council is acknowledging that the map was useful. Is acknowledging the map the same as taking credit for the territory?"

Sora looked at the letter. The ceremony was in two months. The citation read: *for sustained contribution to the understanding of magical practice and its human cost*.

"Is it strange," she said, "to be recognised for the thing you couldn't stop doing?"

"Yes," Tobin said. "Most recognition is."

"Does that make it more meaningful or less?"

"I think it makes it accurate," he said. "Which is better than either."

She picked the letter up. She read it again.

"Will you come to the ceremony?" she asked.

"Was that actually in question?"

"Isn't everything?" she said.

"No," said Tobin. "Not that."

Sora folded the letter carefully along its original crease. Outside the window the students were crossing the courtyard in the afternoon light, and the bells somewhere distant were doing what bells do when the air moves through them.

"Isn't it enough," she said, half to herself, "that the work was worth doing? Does it need to be anything else?"

Tobin was quiet for a moment. Then: "It can be both."

"Can it?" she said.

"Yes," he said. "Let it be."
write a scene where a student of Sora's makes the same mistake she did — pushing past the safe limit. how does she handle it?

**The Mirror**

The student's name was Yeva, and she was twenty and brilliant and had Sora's particular quality of treating the toll as a negotiation rather than a boundary, which was the thing Sora had spent twelve years of her own practice learning was not correct.

She found Yeva on the practice hall floor, which was where Sora herself had been found more than once, and crouched beside her with the measured calm she used when she needed to think clearly about something that was making her stomach move.

"Are you conscious?" Sora said.

"Yes," Yeva said, from the floor. Her voice was present and embarrassed.

"Are your hands working?"

Yeva tested them. "Mostly."

"Is mostly a medical term you're using or a real assessment?"

"Left hand is mostly. Right is fine."

Sora looked at the notation Yeva had left on the practice board — the planned working, the amendments she'd made to it mid-session, the third amendment that had clearly been the one that exceeded the margin. She looked at it for a long time.

"Isn't that my handwriting in the margin?" she said.

Yeva was quiet.

"Did you find my old practice notes?"

"They're in the archive," Yeva said. "They're not restricted."

"Isn't the question why you thought following my old practice notes was a good idea, given what you know about what those notes eventually produced?"

Another silence.

"Can you sit up?" Sora asked.

Yeva sat up, careful. The left hand was cooperating at about seventy percent — Sora could see it in the way she used it as a prop rather than a grip.

"Is there something in that work that felt worth the margin?"

"Yes," Yeva said. Honest, not defensive.

"Isn't that always the answer?" Sora said. "Isn't that exactly what I said, every single time, until it was enough times that the answer stopped being enough?"

Yeva looked at her.

"I'm not telling you the work wasn't worth it," Sora said. "I'm asking you to be able to answer me in thirty years. Can you tell me right now, with honesty, that you'll be able to answer me in thirty years?"

Yeva thought about this seriously, the way good students did.

"I don't know," she said.

"Isn't that the correct answer?" Sora said. "Isn't that why we document it? So the not-knowing has a record?"

She held out her limited right hand. Yeva took it and Sora helped her to her feet.

"Come to the healer," Sora said. "Then we're going to write this up together, every detail, and it's going to go in the archive next to my notes, so that whoever comes after you has both."

"Is that a punishment?" Yeva asked.

"Isn't it obvious that it isn't?" Sora said.
beautiful. write a very short final scene — just Sora alone with her thoughts at the end of a long day

**Evening**

The office was quiet by the time she got to it, which was the thing she liked best about evenings — the particular quality of a room after it has held a full day of people and questions and the slow work of teaching, and then emptied.

She sat at the desk. She did not open any of the three reports waiting there. She looked at the window, where the sky had gone the colour of embers, and the bells of the academy's clock tower were doing their evening count.

Her right hand rested on the desk. Sixty-three percent hearing. Peripheral vision: stable. Right hand: limited range but functional. The healer's words, memorized the way she had memorized everything that mattered.

Outside the window, a student crossed the courtyard below — late, hurrying, clutching a book. Seventeen, perhaps. The age she had been when she walked through Mireth's open gate for the first time with her staff in both hands and her eyes moving fast across everything.

She watched the student until they disappeared around the corner of the north tower.

Then she sat with the quiet for a while.

Was it enough? The question arrived the way all her questions did, without ceremony. Was the map she had made of the territory useful? Had the cost been commensurate?

She looked at her hands. At the window. At the last of the ember-coloured light going blue above the academy rooftops.

Isn't that always the question? she thought.

Isn't the asking of it the answer?
write a scene where someone tries to cure Sora's accumulated toll damage — and she declines

**The Offer**

Corvin came to her office three months after the toll-mapping project with his sister had begun, which had been the most important research development in her field in a decade, and he stood in the doorway with the particular posture of someone who had worked up to something.

"May I try something?" he said.

Sora looked up from her work. "Is may I the right question, or is will you?"

He smiled slightly. He had learned, in three months, how to have this kind of conversation. "Will you let me try to reverse some of the accumulated toll damage?"

She set her pen down. "Is this something you've done before?"

"With my sister. The process is — we think the cost flows between practitioners and attached non-practitioners. If that's true, the reverse flow might be possible."

"Have you tested it?"

"On smaller accumulated tolls. Yes."

Sora looked at him for a long moment. She looked at her right hand, which was its usual sixty-three percent of cooperative. At the window, at the courtyard, at the students moving through the morning.

"Can I ask why you're offering this to me specifically?" she said.

"Because you found the question," Corvin said. "Because my sister is better, and that happened because of this conversation in your classroom. And because the toll you've accumulated is — considerable."

"Isn't considerable a diplomatic word for what Priya's forms actually say?"

He was quiet.

"Is the toll reversible for everyone who has accumulated this much?" Sora asked.

"We don't know. You'd be the most complex case we've tried."

"And if the reversal worked — would it affect my understanding of what the cost is? Would I know less about it because I had less of it?"

Corvin paused. "I don't think knowledge is stored in the damage."

"Isn't it, though?" Sora said. "Doesn't every limit I've found exist because I found it by reaching it? Didn't every course I teach exist because I spent something to understand it?"

He was quiet for a long time.

"Are you refusing?" he said.

"Is that what it sounds like?" Sora said. "Or does it sound like I'm asking whether the thing being offered is the thing I actually want?"

"What do you want?"

She looked at her hand again. At the familiar range of motion, the known limits, the geography of twelve years of commensurate cost.

"Isn't what I want to keep teaching what I know?" she said. "And isn't what I know precisely this?" She held up the hand. "Would you take your sister's illness back if it meant she couldn't find you?"

Corvin looked at her for a long time. "No," he said finally.

"Isn't that the same answer?" Sora said.

He nodded, slowly. Then he said: "Can I at least address the bilateral hearing? It's not functionally necessary to the knowledge."

Sora considered this. It was, she admitted, a reasonable distinction.

"Is sixty-three percent the figure we'd be starting from?" she said.

"Yes."

"And is the goal something above seventy?"

"Ideally."

She looked at the window. At the students. At the bells she could hear imperfectly and had been hearing imperfectly for years.

"Can we discuss it?" she said.

"Yes," he said.

"Isn't that already something?" Sora said.
write a scene where Sora talks to Fen — the young student from the final scene — a few months in, checking in on her progress

**Fen, Three Months In**

Sora found her in the library, which was where Fen went when she was thinking too hard and wanted to look like she was doing something else. Sora recognised this because she had done the same thing at fifteen and possibly still did.

"Is the text you're not reading interesting?" Sora asked, sitting across from her.

Fen looked up. After three months she had learned not to frown at the questions — she had started, instead, doing what Sora suspected she had always done, which was answering them literally.

"It's about historic ward-failures," Fen said. "The Kethlan collapse. I am reading it. Just slowly."

"Is slow the problem, or is the Kethlan collapse not the thing actually on your mind?"

Fen set the book down. "My first solo casting is next week."

"Is next week the thing on your mind, or is the casting?"

"Both."

"Isn't both more honest than either one alone?"

"I keep thinking about the toll," Fen said. "In your course you said — you said it's not a punishment. But it still feels like one."

Sora looked at her. "Does it feel like a punishment because you've done something wrong, or because something is being taken from you without your consent?"

Fen was quiet, considering. "The second one."

"Isn't that the thing I most want you to sit with? That the toll is not consent and is not punishment — it simply is. Doesn't it feel different when you stop trying to categorise it?"

"I don't know yet."

"Is not knowing yet the same as not knowing?"

"No," Fen said slowly. "I suppose it's more like — I know the question. I just don't have the answer from my own experience."

"Isn't that the whole work of the first year?" Sora said. "Getting the experience to put under the theory?"

Fen looked at her hands. Then: "Did you ever feel afraid of the toll?"

Sora considered this carefully. "Isn't the more accurate answer that I was too interested in it to leave room for fear? That the wanting-to-understand was bigger than the afraid?"

"Is that what you'd want for me?"

"Isn't what I'd want for you to find whatever is bigger than the afraid, and let that be the thing that leads?"

Fen thought about this for a long time. Then she picked up the book.

"The Kethlan collapse," she said. "Is that relevant to first solo castings?"

"Isn't knowing what went wrong always relevant to trying to get it right?" Sora said.

"Yes," said Fen. And she kept reading.
write a scene where Sora writes something — a letter, maybe to a future student she'll never meet

**The Letter**

She wrote it at sixty-one, when the toll course had been formally adopted as a standard curriculum requirement across five affiliated academies and Priya had told her with characteristic directness that the bilateral hearing work with Corvin had brought her to seventy-nine percent, which was better than projected.

She wrote it by hand, which she preferred for things that mattered, and she wrote it to no one specific — addressed *To whoever finds this useful*, which was the kind of address that committed to nothing and therefore opened everything.

She wrote:

*Is the toll fair? No. Is it preventable? Sometimes. Is it the reason the work matters? No — the work matters because of what it does, not what it costs. But the cost is the proof that something real happened, and real things leave marks, and the marks are worth knowing.*

*Isn't the question always whether the knowing was worth it? And isn't the answer always yes, because the alternative to knowing is not-knowing, and not-knowing while spending is the only thing I would call a genuine waste?*

*I have spent a great deal. I do not know if I spent it wisely, only that I spent it with attention. Isn't attention all that can actually be asked?*

*If you are reading this because you are afraid of the cost: isn't that the correct response to the right information? Be afraid if you need to be. Then decide.*

*If you are reading this because you have already paid something and are wondering whether it was worth it: doesn't the fact that you're asking mean you're still here? Isn't still here enough to work with?*

*I am not the person who can tell you whether your particular cost was worth your particular work. That answer lives entirely inside you and the people you spent it on and the distance between where you were and where you are.*

*What I can tell you is this: isn't the work always the thing that remains after the cost? Doesn't it outlast what it took?*

*I hope it does. I believe it does. Isn't believing a thing the closest a question can get to being certain?*

She signed it with her name and the date and the toll assessment year — the eleventh, which was the last one that had frightened her, and the first one she had been able to read without flinching — and she folded it and put it in the archive where anyone might find it.

Then she went to teach the afternoon session, and the courtyard was full of students, and the bells were doing what bells do.
write a short scene — Kael in his final years, a brief conversation with Sora

**Kael, Late**

He was ninety-three and still attended the quarterly staff meetings, which Sora considered both admirable and infuriating in equal measure because he was the only person in the room who had known her when she was seventeen with blistered hands and no peripheral vision on the left side, and the meetings always took on a different quality when he was present.

She found him in the old garden — the one the academy had built around the courtyard's east wall, which had been his suggestion twenty years ago and which no one had thought to question because it was Kael.

"Is the garden the reason you're still here?" she asked, sitting beside him.

He looked at it for a moment. The herbs were doing what herbs do in late autumn, which is mostly going dormant with an air of moral superiority. "Possibly," he said.

"Is possibly your way of saying yes?"

"It's my way of saying I don't examine my reasons at ninety-three."

Sora smiled. "Can I ask something I should have asked years ago?"

"You can ask anything. I may not answer."

"Was sending me to Mireth something you knew would produce what it produced? Or was it a routine assignment that happened to contain everything?"

Kael was quiet for a long time. The garden was quiet too, the way late-autumn gardens are — not dead, just waiting.

"Does it matter?" he said.

"Isn't that a deflection from a man who told me deflection was evasion?"

He looked at her. His eyes, which had always given the impression of having already processed what they were seeing, were exactly the same as they had always been.

"I sent you because you were ready," he said. "Not for what you found. For finding something. The rest was yours."

"Isn't that the same as saying you knew?"

"No," he said. "It's saying I trusted."

Sora sat with this. The bells counted the afternoon hour.

"Is there anything you'd want me to tell them?" she said. "The students. Anything you haven't already said."

Kael considered the garden for a long time.

"Tell them," he said finally, "that the questions matter more than the answers. That you spent a lifetime proving it. That the proof is in the archive and also in the room every time you teach."

"Didn't you just describe my entire curriculum?" Sora said.

"Wasn't that the point?" said Kael, which was the closest he had ever come, in her presence, to a smile.
write one truly final scene — Sora very old, passing the toll course to a successor. who is it?

**Succession**

It was Fen.

This did not surprise Sora, which was perhaps the thing that surprised her most about it. She had watched Fen for fifteen years — from the courtyard on the first autumn morning to the solo casting the following week to the graduate work to the three years of teaching alongside her, and the thing that had always been true about Fen was that she asked better questions the longer she worked, which was the reverse of most people and which was, Sora had long since concluded, the only quality that actually mattered.

They sat in the office — Sora's office, which would become Fen's office in the formal sense at the end of term but which Fen had already begun to inhabit in the informal sense that mattered more.

"Is there anything you want to tell me," Fen said, "that isn't in the course notes?"

Sora looked at the window. At the courtyard. At the autumn light that was the same light as the day Fen arrived, adjusted for fifteen years of the same angle.

"Isn't everything that matters already in the course notes?"

"That's not an answer."

"Isn't it?"

Fen looked at her with the expression she had developed over fifteen years, which was the one that meant she was going to wait and Sora might as well say the thing.

"Can I ask what frightens you most about it?" Sora said.

"The same thing that frightened you," Fen said. "That I'll get it wrong. That I'll push a student toward something I shouldn't. That I'll miss the moment."

"Didn't I get it wrong?"

"Several times," Fen said. "Yeva's file. The practice hall incident with the ward-array. The eastern ward-line decision that Tobin had to stop."

"And doesn't the course still exist? Isn't it better for having the wrong versions documented?"

"Yes."

"Then isn't getting it wrong the thing that makes the course real rather than theoretical?"

Fen was quiet for a moment. Then: "Is that what you'd tell the student version of me? That getting it wrong is the point?"

"Isn't the question whether you learn from it?" Sora said. "Isn't the question whether it goes in the archive?"

Fen smiled — the real one, the one she mostly kept for conversations that mattered. "I'm going to miss the way you teach."

"Aren't I still here?"

"You're retiring."

"Isn't retirement a change of rate rather than a cessation?" Sora said. "Doesn't sustainable mean something I can keep doing rather than something I have to stop?"

Fen looked at her for a long time. Then she said: "Do you know what the first thing I'll tell them is? On the first day?"

"Can I guess?"

"Go ahead."

"Is it that the toll is not a punishment?" Sora said.

"Yes," said Fen. "And then I'm going to tell them how I know. And the answer is going to be you."

Sora looked at the courtyard for a long time. The students moved through the morning light. The bells did what bells do.

"Isn't that," she said finally, "the best kind of answer to the best kind of question?"

"Yes," said Fen. "I think it is."
